title: Data Analysis: Descriptive, Inferential & Qualitative
simple_html:
Data analysis is how raw data becomes an answer. There are three broad jobs: describe what you have, infer what it means for a larger population, and — for words rather than numbers — interpret meaning.

Descriptive statistics — summarise

These summarise your sample. Measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) tell you the typical value; measures of spread (range, standard deviation) tell you how varied the data is. Charts and frequency tables also describe data. Descriptive stats make no claims beyond the data in front of you.

Inferential statistics — generalise

These let you draw conclusions about a whole population from a sample. You state a hypothesis, run a test, and get a p-value. If p is below your threshold (usually 0.05), the result is "statistically significant" — unlikely to be due to chance. Common tests include the t-test (compare two group means), ANOVA (more than two groups), correlation, and chi-square (categories).

Qualitative analysis — interpret

When data is text (interviews, open answers), you look for patterns of meaning. Thematic analysis — coding the text and grouping codes into themes — is the most common approach.

Key takeaways

  • Describe first, then infer.
  • A p-value tells you about chance, not importance — check effect size too.
  • The right test depends on your data type and question.
  • Qualitative analysis finds themes; quantitative analysis tests hypotheses.
  • Statistical significance is not the same as practical significance.
intermediate_html:
Data analysis converts collected data into evidence and answers. The toolkit splits into descriptive statistics (summarising the sample), inferential statistics (generalising to a population), and qualitative analysis (interpreting non-numeric data). Choosing correctly depends on your research question and your variables' measurement levels (Lesson 6).

Descriptive statistics

These characterise the data at hand. Central tendency: mean (average, for interval/ratio), median (middle value, robust to outliers), mode (most frequent, works for nominal). Dispersion: range, variance, standard deviation, and interquartile range. Shape: skewness and kurtosis. Visual summaries — histograms, box plots, bar charts — reveal distribution and outliers before any testing.

Inferential statistics

Inference uses sample data to make probabilistic claims about a population. The logic of null hypothesis significance testing: state a null (H0: no effect) and alternative (H1: effect), choose a significance level (alpha, usually 0.05), compute a test statistic and p-value, and reject H0 if p < alpha. Beware the two error types: Type I (false positive — rejecting a true null) and Type II (false negative — missing a real effect). Always report effect size (e.g. Cohen's d, r-squared), because significance with a large sample can be trivially small in practice.

Choosing the right test

QuestionDataTest
Difference, 2 groupsInterval/ratio DVIndependent t-test
Difference, 3+ groupsInterval/ratio DVANOVA
Relationship, 2 continuousInterval/ratioPearson correlation
Relationship, categoricalNominalChi-square
PredictionInterval/ratio DVRegression

Parametric tests (t, ANOVA, Pearson) assume normality and interval data; if violated, use non-parametric equivalents (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis, Spearman).

Qualitative analysis

For text data, the dominant method is thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2006): familiarise, generate codes, search for themes, review, define, and report. Alternatives include content analysis (often quantifying frequencies), grounded theory (building theory from data via constant comparison), and narrative or discourse analysis. Rigour comes from the trustworthiness criteria of Lesson 7.

Worked example

You test whether remote workers are more engaged. Engagement (interval, two groups: remote vs office) calls for an independent t-test. Remote group mean = 5.6, office = 4.9; t(178) = 2.8, p = 0.006 — significant at 0.05. Cohen's d = 0.42 (a small-to-medium effect), so the difference is real and modestly meaningful. How to apply it: first identify your DV's measurement level and the number/type of groups, then pick the matching test, check its assumptions, and report both the p-value and the effect size.
full_html:
Data analysis is the stage at which collected data is transformed into findings. It encompasses three complementary domains: descriptive statistics that summarise a dataset, inferential statistics that license generalisation from sample to population, and qualitative analysis that interprets meaning in non-numeric data. The analytic choice is not free: it is constrained upstream by philosophy and design (Lessons 1–4), by sampling (Lesson 5), and decisively by the measurement levels established in data collection (Lesson 6).

Descriptive statistics

Descriptive statistics characterise the sample without inferential claims. Central tendency comprises the mean (appropriate for interval/ratio data and the basis of most parametric methods), the median (the positional middle, robust to skew and outliers), and the mode (the most frequent value, the only valid central measure for nominal data). Dispersion is captured by the range, variance, standard deviation, and the interquartile range. Distributional shape is described by skewness and kurtosis, which matter because many inferential tests assume approximate normality. Exploratory data analysis (Tukey, 1977) stresses visual inspection — histograms, box plots, scatterplots — before formal testing, to detect outliers, non-linearity, and errors that would otherwise corrupt inference.

Inferential statistics and the logic of hypothesis testing

Inference reasons from a sample to a population under uncertainty. The dominant paradigm is null hypothesis significance testing (NHST), a hybrid of Fisher's significance testing and the Neyman–Pearson decision framework. One states a null hypothesis (H0: no effect/relationship) and an alternative (H1), selects a significance level alpha (conventionally 0.05), computes a test statistic and its p-value — the probability of data at least as extreme as observed if H0 were true — and rejects H0 when p < alpha. Two decision errors arise: Type I (rejecting a true null — a false positive, probability alpha) and Type II (failing to reject a false null — a false negative, probability beta). Statistical power (1 − beta) is the probability of detecting a real effect and rises with sample size, effect size, and alpha (Cohen, 1988).

A critical and widely misunderstood point: statistical significance is not practical significance. With a large enough sample, trivially small effects become significant. Hence the modern consensus — reinforced by the American Statistical Association's statement on p-values (Wasserstein & Lazar, 2016) — is to report effect sizes (Cohen's d, r, eta-squared, R-squared) and confidence intervals alongside, or instead of, bare p-values.

Choosing the right test

Test selection follows from the research question, the number and nature of variables, and their measurement levels:

GoalVariablesParametric testNon-parametric
Compare 2 independent groupsContinuous DV, categorical IVIndependent t-testMann–Whitney U
Compare 2 related measuresContinuous, pairedPaired t-testWilcoxon signed-rank
Compare 3+ groupsContinuous DVANOVAKruskal–Wallis
Association, 2 continuousInterval/ratioPearson rSpearman rho
Association, categoricalNominal—Chi-square
Predict an outcomeContinuous DVLinear regressionLogistic (binary DV)

Parametric tests assume interval/ratio data, approximate normality, and (for several) homogeneity of variance; when these are violated, the non-parametric alternatives, which rely on ranks, are preferred. Regression deserves emphasis as the workhorse of business research: it estimates the effect of one or more predictors on an outcome, yielding coefficients, R-squared (variance explained), and significance tests, while requiring attention to assumptions such as linearity, independence of errors, homoscedasticity, and absence of multicollinearity.

Qualitative data analysis

Qualitative analysis interprets meaning in textual or visual data and is aligned with interpretivist philosophy. Thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2006) is the most widely used: a six-phase process of familiarisation, initial coding, searching for themes, reviewing themes, defining and naming, and reporting. Content analysis systematically categorises and often quantifies textual features, bridging qualitative and quantitative logics. Grounded theory (Glaser & Strauss, 1967) builds theory inductively through open, axial, and selective coding with constant comparison until theoretical saturation. Narrative analysis examines stories and their structure, while discourse analysis studies how language constructs social reality. Analytic rigour is demonstrated through the trustworthiness criteria (Lincoln & Guba, 1985) introduced in Lesson 7, and increasingly via CAQDAS tools such as NVivo. Mixed-methods designs integrate qualitative and quantitative strands to offset the limits of each.

Worked example

A study asks whether remote workers report higher engagement than office workers. The dependent variable (engagement) is continuous and the independent variable has two independent groups, so an independent-samples t-test is appropriate. Remote: mean = 5.6, SD = 0.9; office: mean = 4.9, SD = 1.0; t(178) = 2.84, p = 0.005, so H0 is rejected at alpha = 0.05. Reporting effect size, Cohen's d = 0.42, indicates a small-to-medium effect — statistically significant and modestly meaningful. A 95% confidence interval on the mean difference of [0.21, 1.19] excludes zero, corroborating the result. Had engagement been measured as a single ordinal item or violated normality, the Mann–Whitney U would replace the t-test.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Misinterpreting the p-value as the probability that the null is true, or that the result is important — it is neither.
  • Ignoring effect size and confidence intervals, reporting significance alone.
  • Choosing a test that violates measurement-level or distributional assumptions (e.g. a t-test on nominal data).
  • p-hacking / HARKing — running many tests or hypothesising after results are known, inflating Type I error.
  • Confusing correlation with causation in observational data.
  • Forcing qualitative data into premature counts, losing the interpretive depth that justified collecting it.

Cross-links

This capstone lesson depends on every prior layer of the research onion: the question and philosophy (Lessons 1–2) set the analytic logic; the design and strategy (Lessons 3–4) determine whether causal inference is licensed; sampling (Lesson 5) governs generalisability and power; and measurement (Lesson 6) and the reliability/validity checks (Lesson 7) determine which analyses are legitimate and how far conclusions can be trusted.
